item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quaint, and I like the atmosphere enough to upvote. That being said, I think the Wikiwalk crosslinks at the end could be clearer, particularly in how two locations are crosstested. Also, I think that'd probably work at an earlier line, probably right after the sun/moon perception swap is first mentioned. It really blunts from the ending where it currently is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read it a bit about the topic, and I think I will stick with 'fortnightly'. 'Bi-weekly' seems a bit ambiguous, as to if it means 'twice per week' or 'once every two weeks'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think fortnightly should be changed to bi-weekly. As much as I enjoy the word, it's a bit antiquated and not normally used in a scientific context.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a chance to talk to MrWrong about this one, and it came up that SCP-2248's effect would also work on SCP-2995 and SCP-1812, and would not work on SCP-756 or SCP-1483 As such, I am recommending the following edits that MrWrong suggested:

Changing this:

Subjects are able to see under low light conditions when the Moon is visible in the night sky, and are susceptible to retinopathy through direct observation of the Moon. In addition, erythema is common among subjects who have prolonged exposure to moonlight.

To this:

Subjects are able to see under low light conditions when the Moon is visible in the night sky, and are susceptible to retinopathy through direct observation of the Moon. In addition, erythema is common among subjects who have prolonged exposure to moonlight. When subjects observe SCP-1812, the probabilities of retinopathy and/or heatstroke are significantly increased due to [REDACTED].

Changing this:

In contrast, subjects experience visual impairment on nights with a new moon, during daytime or during a lunar eclipse. They are also capable of viewing the Sun directly without detriment, claiming that the Sun is perpetually in a solar eclipse. In addition, they do not suffer from erythema or other symptoms of sunburn despite prolonged exposure to sunlight.

To this:

In contrast, subjects experience visual impairment on nights with a new moon, during daytime or during a lunar eclipse. They are also capable of viewing the Sun directly without detriment, claiming that the Sun is perpetually in a solar eclipse. In addition, they do not suffer from erythema or other symptoms of sunburn despite prolonged exposure to sunlight. The aforementioned traits are similarly present when subjects observe SCP-2995, but not the Suns in SCP-756 or selected extra-dimensional locations (e.g. Universe B-10208-Alpha-1483).

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Re: Update 1
item.content:encoded: Nah, you weren't harsh. You offered your opinion, which I looked into and improved the writing based on it.

item.title: Re: Update 1
item.content:encoded: This is definitely better. Sorry if I was too harsh before, it's just that addendums that explain everything have always been a pet peeve of mine and have been increasingly common as of late.

item.title: Update 1
item.content:encoded: Have I not? There were a few ideas I wanted to convey in the commentary, but I've relocated them into the text found inside the temple/shrine. Hope it will be less on-the-nose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was good until the addendum at the end where you explained everything in plain english. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, thanks for the ad hoc look-through. Saw your suggestion and reworked it into the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a few nitpicks - sorry for not picking up in the drafts:

+ Expand
– hide block

a temple complex with an area of approximately 20 m2

While a 4m x 5m structure isn't a small shrine, I have difficulty accepting it as a temple complex with a central chamber. Even going up to 200m2 would still only be around 20m x 10m, which is small enough to hide on the island, and you wouldn't have to make it that large.

A sign in front of SCP-2248 labels the site as "Templum Luna Primogenita".

This was jarring both from the use of the phrase "sign in front" (which suggests a museum label - I'm not aware of that kind of practice in Roman temples generally) and the idea that a persecuted cult wanting to worship their sacred statue in secret would stick a sign on the front for helpful reference.

One possible solution is to delete the sentence entirely. It's clear by the end of the draft what the temple is. If you want to give readers a helpful signpost, however, perhaps you could replace the sign in front of the temple with an inscription on the temple ("Gloria lunae primogenitus" or something that actually has correct Latin grammar).

I feel like the section on plants could be streamlined, as I found it a significant speedbump while reading. Perhaps:

When initially discovered, the plant species Amaranthus blitum (purple amaranth) and Cupressus sempervirens (Mediterranean cypress) were found in SCP-2248. Despite SCP-2248 possessing suitable conditions for plant growth, seeds from at least 20 other species were found ungerminated. Radiocarbon dating indicates that the oldest seeds present are dated to circa 200 – 260 CE.1

Plant species from outside the genus Amaranthus and Cupressus that are replanted into SCP-2248 show no signs of growth and will gradually wither. Notably, plants under the genus Myrtus and Laurus and their seeds will undergo spontaneous combustion within 24 hours of being placed into SCP-2248.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay! I do agree that it is different enough to stand up, with the historical context. It was just similar enough to be on my mind.

Sorry if I got ranty there. I think the statement about photography in the footnote was helpful, but I'm not dictating.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addressing point by point:

  1. Never read 1008 before, so I wasn't aware of any similarities (if any). Although the two articles seem to have enough differences to stand out.
  2. I can guess you are right. I'm going to change that bit to being "sunburnt" by the Moon.
  3. Makes sense now that you mention it.
  4. Point taken.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but I have some problems with it.

+ show block
– hide block

1) It's main solar effect seems like a watered-down version of SCP-1008. It's interesting that the fisherman experience physiological consequences from moonlight (although I'm not clear on how that happens, more below), although attributing physical maladies to it… also leans back towards 1008. I think the historical angle is interesting enough to lend this some weight, but it's still mighty similar.

Also,

altering the sensory perception of the Moon and Sun for individuals who have observed SCP-2248-A or an image of it (hereon referred to as subjects).

Seems redundant, given that you immediately describe those effects.

2) Similarly, the description of the blinding of the fishermen seemed like a repetition of the section on moonlight, although I'm very unclear on how they were blinded. I mean, if moonlight essentially becomes as effective as sunlight for them, wouldn't their reflexes to occasionally shade their eyes, blink, or not stare at the Sun/Moon just adjust? I mean, those are generally automatic, physiological responses.

3) Tense issue:

When initially discovered, the plant species… are found in SCP-2248. Seeds… are found as well,

"When initially discovered" indicating a past event, followed by current-tense verbs. Drove me batty.

4)

SCP-2248-A is classified as a Type DV cognitohazard

I'm all for crafting new terminology, but when I scrolled down to see that it just meant "Direct Visual," it felt like a waste of my effort. It seemed like something that didn't need to be summarized, like a useless bureaucratic gesture rather than a helpful designation for a complex effect.

This isn't something like Type 2 Poltergeist, which suggests a vast expertise on something the author doesn't need to expound. I'd be more comfortable with something like IINDV Cognitohazard for "Immediate Isolated Non-reversible Direct Visual." Just some kind of demonstration that you're really packing in the information, rather than shifting half a sentence about photography into a footnote.

5) Positive point, I can imagine all kinds of narratives arising from this, which is great. Legionary and priestly adherents of Sol Invictus dragged before the statue to deprive them of their God. Members of the cult stealthily overtaking Legion positions at night, only to be slaughtered wholesale in their blinded condition in the following daylight. Neat stuff.


Ok, so, ultimately no-vote for now. I like it, but there are enough things bothering me to prevent an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for catching that. Amended accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Footnote two implies that for a DV class cognitohazard does not affect someone when viewed through pictures, but the sentence immediately following says that viewing photos of the statue also causes the effect to occur, is there a typo?

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attribution, A few words and Other matters
item.content:encoded: And after I spend 20 minutes finding the island on a map to add this to my SCP Map, you just give the island's name in the comments… proves I should read them first. Anyway, I like this, take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Admittedly, there is "some attempt" of a human aspect in the commentary. While not explicit, the author is a Christian taking down notes on pagan mysteries at a time when they are soon to be wiped out.

It's hard to have a human aspect to the thing, when you decide that it should be consigned to the ash-heaps of history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd still like to see some human aspect to all this, even if it's in the form of a Tale, but this is still decent enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the Luna Primogenotia cult and it is plausible to me that if such a cult existed in the late empire, it might have conflated itself with the Dianic mysteries, just as the Christian, Sol Invictus and Mithraic cults did with other pagan mysteries.

Anyway +1.

item.title: Thanks, Attribution, A few words and Other matters
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to a variety of people such as Fae, TwistedGears, Minmin, Lexnat0r, LadyKatie etc. who have seen it and said their piece of it.

The image used here is attributed to Khayman of Wikipedia (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ze%C4%8Da) via CC BY SA 3.0.

And a few other words to say:

+ show block
– hide block

While it is true that Diana/Artemis ≠ Luna/Selene and Apollo ≠ Sol/Helios (strictly speaking), their similar domains tend to have these deities mixed into each other. And going by interpretatio romana, people tend to select aspects of their deities to form some level of coherency. The founders of Luna Primogenita likely saw a bit of Apollo and Sol in the sun deity pitched by Elagabalus and Aurelian.

I did play with the moon cult being alive to this day, but I thought back to all the SCPs that involve cults and I think we need a dead cult for once.

P.S. The ending implies that the cult sacrificed themselves to prevent the statue to be destroyed by the legions or Christians, in an effort to preserve the main aspect of their faith. The people are killed, but the idea remains.
